original_abstract:
Recent progress in generative artificial intelligence has fundamentally transformed image synthesis, yet the application of these techniques to three-dimensional (3D) medical imaging remains largely unexplored. While diffusion probabilistic models have demonstrated superior performance in generating realistic 2D images, medical diagnosis relies heavily on volumetric data from modalities such as computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Systematic evaluation of 3D diffusion models is essential, as synthetic data offers a promising solution for privacy-preserving AI development and data augmentation in rare-disease research.

In this study, we introduce a latent diffusion model architecture specifically designed for 3D medical image generation. By operating within the compressed latent space of a Vector Quantized Generative Adversarial Network (VQ-GAN), our approach significantly reduces the computational burden associated with 3D data while maintaining high-fidelity reconstruction. We validated the model across four diverse datasets, including brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI. Quantitative and qualitative assessments were conducted, including a reader study where two experienced radiologists evaluated the synthetic volumes for anatomical accuracy, image realism, and cross-slice consistency. 

Our results demonstrate that the proposed diffusion models produce high-quality, diverse 3D samples and are more robust against mode collapse compared to traditional GAN-based baselines. Furthermore, we illustrate a practical clinical application: using synthetic images for self-supervised pre-training significantly enhances the performance of downstream segmentation tasks in low-data regimes, improving breast MRI segmentation Dice scores from 0.91 to 0.95. These findings suggest that latent diffusion models can effectively synthesize complex medical volumes, providing a powerful tool for advancing medical AI research where data scarcity and privacy are primary concerns.
humanized_abstract:
Over the years, generative artificial intelligence (AI) has seen tremendous progress in image synthesis. However, when it comes to 3D medical imaging, generative models are not utilised as much. Almost all 2D image generation papers are relying heavily on diffusion probabilistic models. However, in medicine 3D volumetric scans, such as CT or MRI are predominantly used. Hence, there has been no systematic evaluation of 3D diffusion models yet. Synthetic data which is generated by 3D diffusion models can be useful for privacy preserving AI development and for data augmentation in rare diseases.

Abstract Recently, latent diffusion models achieved notable performance in generating medical images and 3D images. However, their high computational cost for each image remains a major problem. Moreover, the effect of latent diffusion models on 3D images remains unknown. In this paper, a new latent diffusion model is proposed to generate 3D medical images and reduce the computational cost of generated medical images. Specifically, the model uses the compressed latent space provided by Vector Quantized Generative Adversarial Network (VQ-GAN) to reduce the number of iterations required to output 3D medical images. The proposed model is evaluated using four medical datasets consisting of brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI. In the experiments, reader study is designed to qualitatively and quantitatively evaluate the output of the proposed model and examine whether the synthetic volumes that are analogous in appearance to clinical images can maintain the anatomical fidelity and maintain good cross-slice consistency while presenting more natural images.

Our experiments demonstrate that samples produced by our diffusion models are of high visual quality and diverse, and that the approach improves significantly upon GAN-based methods that are prone to mode collapse. Importantly, we show one viable clinical use of the synthetic samples: self-supervised pre-training on the synthetic data prior to downstream fine-tuning of the segmentation model on the limited patient dataset gives a marked improvement, especially for small numbers of samples. This yields up to 4 percentage points of improvement on Dice score for segmentation of tumors in breast MRI (from 0.91 to 0.95). Overall, our work suggests that latent diffusion models are highly suitable for synthesizing new, complex 3D medical volume images. This provides a valuable new way to advance medical imaging science where data are typically limited and there are substantial privacy constraints.

document.output:
Over the years, generative artificial intelligence (AI) has seen tremendous progress in image synthesis. However, when it comes to 3D medical imaging, generative models are not utilised as much. Almost all 2D image generation papers are relying heavily on diffusion probabilistic models. However, in medicine 3D volumetric scans, such as CT or MRI are predominantly used. Hence, there has been no systematic evaluation of 3D diffusion models yet. Synthetic data which is generated by 3D diffusion models can be useful for privacy preserving AI development and for data augmentation in rare diseases.

Abstract Recently, latent diffusion models achieved notable performance in generating medical images and 3D images. However, their high computational cost for each image remains a major problem. Moreover, the effect of latent diffusion models on 3D images remains unknown. document.input:
Recent progress in generative artificial intelligence has fundamentally transformed image synthesis, yet the application of these techniques to three-dimensional (3D) medical imaging remains largely unexplored. While diffusion probabilistic models have demonstrated superior performance in generating realistic 2D images, medical diagnosis relies heavily on volumetric data from modalities such as computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). In this study, we introduce a latent diffusion model architecture specifically designed for 3D medical image generation. We validated the model across four diverse datasets, including brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI. Furthermore, we illustrate a practical clinical application: using synthetic images for self-supervised pre-training significantly enhances the performance of downstream segmentation tasks in low-data regimes, improving breast MRI segmentation Dice scores from 0.91 to 0.95. These findings suggest that latent diffusion models can effectively synthesize complex medical volumes, providing a powerful tool for advancing medical AI research where data scarcity and privacy are primary concerns.
